questions.question: What is the primary purpose of Notion Academy as described in the article?

options.text: To sell premium Notion templates
options.explanation: The article mentions template creation, but the primary purpose is broader learning, not sales.

options.text: To help users earn badges and certifications while learning Notion
options.explanation: Correct – the Academy is positioned as a place to learn Notion, earn badges, and get certified.

options.text: To provide a marketplace for third‑party apps
options.explanation: The marketplace is mentioned only for templates, not as the Academy’s main goal.

options.text: To offer free cloud storage for Notion files
options.explanation: Storage isn’t discussed in the article; it focuses on learning resources.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT listed as a type of Notion Academy offering?

options.text: Live webinars
options.explanation: Live webinars are explicitly mentioned.

options.text: Community forums
options.explanation: The article mentions joining the community but not a dedicated forum platform.

options.text: Badged learning paths
options.explanation: Badged paths are a core feature.

options.text: Certification exams
options.explanation: Certification for Notion Admins is highlighted.

questions.question: If a user wants to become a Notion Certified Admin, which statement best reflects the requirements according to the article?

options.text: They must be on Notion’s Enterprise plan.
options.explanation: The article says certification is available for any plan type.

options.text: They need to complete a specific certification path that covers security best practices and workspace scaling.
options.explanation: Correct – the certification focuses on scaling workspaces and security.

options.text: They must submit a portfolio of templates they’ve created.
options.explanation: Template portfolios are unrelated to admin certification.

options.text: They must attend at least three live webinars per month.
options.explanation: Webinar attendance isn’t a stated requirement for certification.

questions.question: Which lesson category is most likely to teach you how to build an intake system using forms, charts, and automations?

options.text: Custom Agents
options.explanation: Custom Agents focus on automation triggers, not specifically intake systems.

options.text: An issue tracker for any team
options.explanation: The description explicitly mentions building an intake system with forms, charts, and automations.

options.text: Workspace design
options.explanation: Workspace design covers layout best practices, not intake forms.

options.text: Templates & marketplace
options.explanation: That lesson is about creating and selling templates, not intake workflows.

questions.question: What distinguishes the "Advanced Path" from the "Essentials Path"?

options.text: It includes a certification exam at the end.
options.explanation: Both paths can lead to badges; the article does not mention a certification exam for the Advanced Path specifically.

options.text: It focuses on automating work and workspace architecture best practices.
options.explanation: Correct – the Advanced Path description mentions automation and advanced capabilities.

options.text: It is only available to Enterprise plan users.
options.explanation: The article does not restrict paths by plan type.

options.text: It contains fewer lessons than the Essentials Path.
options.explanation: The Advanced Path actually has more lessons (5) compared to Essentials (3).

questions.question: Which statement best explains how the "Workflows Path" integrates AI into its curriculum?

options.text: It teaches users to build custom AI chatbots that replace human reviewers.
options.explanation: The article mentions AI assisting workflows, not replacing reviewers entirely.

options.text: It shows how to create connected systems that unite teams and projects, with AI helping to streamline processes.
options.explanation: Correct – the description highlights AI as a helper in building connected systems.

options.text: It focuses on training AI models using Notion data exports.
options.explanation: Training AI models is not mentioned; the focus is on using AI within workflows.

options.text: It requires participants to pass an AI proficiency test before proceeding.
options.explanation: No such test is referenced in the article.

questions.question: If a beginner wants to learn how to share and collaborate in Notion, which lesson should they start with?

options.text: Sharing & collaboration
options.explanation: The lesson title directly matches the beginner’s goal.

options.text: Project management
options.explanation: Project management focuses on tasks and projects, not collaboration basics.

options.text: Custom Agents
options.explanation: Custom Agents are about automation, not sharing features.

options.text: Workspace design
options.explanation: Workspace design covers layout, not the mechanics of sharing.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the relationship between Notion Academy’s “Badged Paths” and “Certificates”?

options.text: Badged Paths award badges for completing lessons, while certificates are earned by passing a separate admin certification exam.
options.explanation: Correct – badges come from paths/lessons; certificates are tied to the Notion Certified Admin program.

options.text: Both badges and certificates are automatically granted after finishing any path.
options.explanation: Certificates require a specific certification process, not just any path completion.

options.text: Certificates replace badges once a learner reaches an advanced level.
options.explanation: Certificates do not replace badges; they are a distinct credential.

options.text: Badged Paths are only for Enterprise customers, while certificates are for all users.
options.explanation: The article does not limit badges to Enterprise users.
